trends.name: LLMs + language
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around language, reasoning, unlearning, with representative work on Breaking the Reversal Curse in Autoregressive Language Models via Identity Bridge.
representativeSentences: Despite the advancing reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs), many reasoning evaluations are increasingly compromised by data contamination, which induces unreliable contaminated reasoning on leaked inputs.
RL-based post-training with GRPO is widely used to improve large language models on individual reasoning tasks.
Large language models (LLMs) can memorize sensitive facts, motivating unlearning methods that remove targeted knowledge without costly retraining.

trends.name: Reinforcement Learning + diffusion
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around diffusion, reward, visual, with representative work on MultiLoReFT: Decoupling Shared and Modality-Specific Subspaces in Multimodal Learning via Low....
representativeSentences: While text-to-image diffusion models achieve remarkable generation quality, they inadvertently memorize sensitive content, necessitating machine unlearning to prevent undesired outputs.
Text-to-image diffusion models achieve impressive visual fidelity, yet they remain unreliable in multi-object generation.
These findings highlight fundamental limitations of diffusion models and motivate stronger inductive biases and data design for robust multi-object compositional generation.

trends.name: Vision + multimodal
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around multimodal, modalities, modality, with representative work on Group Contrastive Learning for Weakly Paired Multimodal Data.
representativeSentences: Hyperbolic geometry has recently emerged as a powerful representation space for multimodal learning, as it naturally captures hierarchical semantic structure across modalities.
Extensive experiments demonstrate that IBMA achieves superior performance compared to existing multimodal learning methods, validating the effectiveness of modality-specific representation alignment.

In this paper, we propose Information Bottleneck–based Multimodal Alignment (IBMA), a novel multimodal learning framework that enforces the IB principle for both the fused multimodal representation and modality-specific representations.

trends.name: Optimization + networks
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around networks, neural, gradient, with representative work on Adaptive Momentum and Nonlinear Damping for Neural Network Training.
representativeSentences: Specifically, we analyze one-hidden-layer quadratic neural networks trained by gradient descent on a sequence of XOR-cluster datasets with Gaussian noise, where different tasks correspond to clusters with orthogonal means.

A plethora of recent works has shown that for wide, overparameterized neural networks, training with Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) often leads to interpolation of the training data without sacrificing generalization performance.

Even for the gradient descent (GD) method applied to neural network training, understanding its optimization dynamics, including convergence rate, iterate trajectories, function value oscillations, and especially its implicit acceleration, remains a challenging problem.

trends.name: Theory + learning
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around learning, federated learning, federated, with representative work on Forgetting Whenever You Want: A Decentralized Continual Learning Framework with On-Demand Unl....
representativeSentences: Federated learning (FL) allows clients to collaboratively train models without exposing private data, but practical FL is simultaneously challenged by data heterogeneity and model heterogeneity.

Federated Learning (FL) faces significant challenges due to domain heterogeneity, where data from different clients exhibit substantial statistical shifts that hinder the generalization of the global model.

Federated learning typically aggregates client updates using fixed or heuristic weighting rules, which can be suboptimal when clients have heterogeneous data and varying contributions to the global model.

trends.name: Systems + unlearning
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around unlearning, learning, federated, with representative work on Learning Generalized Label Distributions.
representativeSentences: Federated learning increasingly faces deletion requests that require client-level unlearning without sacrificing model quality, yet a client’s influence is often deeply entangled after many rounds of aggregation.

Based on this finding, we propose Federated Decoupled LoRA (FDLoRA), which employs a dual-branch mechanism to decouple robust feature learning from noise modeling and mitigates noise interference through noisy branch negative learning.

Experiments across several benchmarks demonstrate that Representation Unlearning achieves more reliable forgetting, better utility retention, and greater computational efficiency than parameter-centric baselines.

trends.name: Safety + uncertainty
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around uncertainty, learning, robust, with representative work on Beyond Point-wise Neural Collapse: A Topology-Aware Hierarchical Classifier for Class-Increme....
representativeSentences: Existing CMH methods often overlook the uncertainty introduced by noise or semantic ambiguity, making models susceptible to overfitting noisy labels and yielding unreliable similarity judgments during inference.

To address this issue, we propose a Robust Self-reflective Hashing (RSH) framework that prudently analyzes semantic discrepancies while accounting for uncertainty, thereby effectively mitigating interference from noisy labels.
Cross-modal Hashing (CMH) typically assumes a perfectly complete data annotation, whereas noisy labels are unavoidable in practical scenarios.

trends.name: Evaluation + censoring
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around censoring, networks, learning, with representative work on On the Power of Source Screening for Learning Shared Feature Extractors.
representativeSentences: In practice, nonlinear neural networks often succeed in extracting such hidden structure in unsupervised and self-supervised learning.

We study an empirical phenomenon in low-rank random-feature networks: even when stochastic training sees one non-symmetrized sample at a time, the learned model can recover a globally symmetric function and, in successful regimes, symmetric internal partial functions.

Existing relational learning methods typically learn static entity representations and assume symmetric relation inference, which can be inadequate for capturing context-dependent entity functions and the inherent asymmetry of high-order relations.

trends.name: Generative Models + generative
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around generative, flow, flow matching, with representative work on Aligning Datasets and Models for Weight Space Learning.
representativeSentences: Then, to find such solutions, we develop an inference framework based on maximum entropy and continuous normalizing flows (CNFs), trained via flow matching.
These structured representations are then modeled using conditional flow matching to enable flexible and generalizable perturbation prediction.

Empirically, applying MacroGuide to pretrained diffusion models increases macrocycle generation rates from 1% to 99%, while matching or exceeding state-of-the-art performance on key quality metrics such as chemical validity, diversity, and PoseBusters checks.

trends.name: Other + mathbf
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around mathbf, exogenous variables, iforest, with representative work on On the Expressive Power of Permutation-Equivariant Weight-Space Networks.
representativeSentences: Weight-space learning studies neural architectures that operate directly on the parameters of other neural networks.

This paper investigates the grokking phenomenon, which refers to the sudden transition from a long memorization to generalization observed during neural networks training, in the context of learning multiplication in finite-dimensional algebras (FDA).
Riemannian neural networks have proven effective in solving a variety of machine learning tasks.

trends.name: Agents + incentive
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around incentive, strategic classification, strategic, with representative work on Toward Identifiable Sparse Autoencoders.
representativeSentences: To formalize this limitation, we identify and define a new problem setting, termed the behaviorally realistic strategic classification problem, where agents’ strategic manipulations deviate from full rationality due to psychological biases.

The rise of auto-bidding in online advertising has created new challenges for ensuring advertiser incentive compatibility, particularly when advertisers delegate bidding to agents with high-level constraints.
Strategic classification studies the interaction between decision models and agents who strategically manipulate their features for favorable outcomes.

trends.name: Probabilistic Methods + follower
trends.summary: This trend groups papers around follower, rnns, buyer, with representative work on A unified theory of feature learning in RNNs and DNNs.
representativeSentences: In DNN-typical tasks, we identify a phase transition when the learning signal overcomes the noise due to randomness in the weights: below this threshold, RNNs and DNNs behave identically; above it, only RNNs develop correlated representations across timesteps.
Recurrent and deep neural networks (RNNs/DNNs) are cornerstone architectures in machine learning.

To address this question, we here develop a unified mean-field theory for RNNs and DNNs in terms of representational kernels, describing fully trained networks in the feature learning ($\mu$P) regime.
